text: When designing a REST API, following best practices ensures it is easy to use and maintain. Endpoints should be clear and logical, often using nouns to represent resources, such as `/users` or `/products`. Use standard HTTP status codes to indicate outcomes: `200` for success, `201` for created resources, `404` for not found, and `500` for server errors. Good documentation is essential for developers to understand how to use your API, so include examples and descriptions. Versioning is also important; add a version number to your endpoints, like `/v1/users`, to allow updates without breaking existing users. These practices make your API reliable and developer-friendly.
